curator: auto (generate_day.py — reals verified verbatim vs source + cross-validated)

quotes.text: You want loyalty? Buy a dog.
quotes.speaker: Chinatown (1974)
quotes.context: Jake Gittes, dismissing a client who questions his motives
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — Gittes talks in this clipped, world-weary PI rhythm and the line fits his cynical persona perfectly. The subtle giveaway is it's just a touch too aphoristic and clean; real Gittes lines are messier and tied to the specific scene.

quotes.text: How could I have known that murder can sometimes smell like honeysuckle?
quotes.speaker: Double Indemnity (1944)
quotes.context: Insurance salesman Walter Neff (Fred MacMurray) narrating his dictaphone confession about the affair and murder plot with Phyllis Dietrichson.

quotes.text: You don't build something like this without making enemies.
quotes.speaker: Citizen Kane (1941)
quotes.context: A colleague reflecting on Kane's rise
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — the film's interview-style structure means lines like this get spoken in retrospect, and the cadence matches the period. The giveaway is it's a little too on-the-nose thematically; Welles's script tends to approach Kane's flaws more obliquely.

quotes.text: Death's at the bottom of everything, Martins. Leave death to the professionals.
quotes.speaker: The Third Man (1949)
quotes.context: Major Calloway (Trevor Howard) to pulp novelist Holly Martins (Joseph Cotten), warning him off his amateur investigation in postwar Vienna.

quotes.text: I'm not the guy you kill, I'm the guy you buy!
quotes.speaker: Michael Clayton (2007)
quotes.context: Michael Clayton (George Clooney) shouting at Karen Crowder near the film's climax, after realizing the conspiracy against him.

quotes.text: I used to think the desert was empty. Turns out it's just patient.
quotes.speaker: Lawrence of Arabia (1962)
quotes.context: Lawrence speaking to a British officer after returning from the Nefud
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — Lawrence's lines often have this quasi-mystical, landscape-obsessed quality and the desert setting is obviously right. The giveaway is 'just patient' personifies the desert a little too neatly; Bolt's script is more oblique about Lawrence's inner states.
